Learn pass done: index was already up to date, and the session contains no corrections, save-requests, reversals, or confirmed approaches to crystallize (my diagnosis isn't yet validated), so nothing was written. Here's the analysis.

## Diagnosis

The highest-leverage fix is **retuning the archive's page granularity — writing much larger pages to object storage** — because the archive-read GET fan-out is the only cost component whose growth is decoupled from ingest volume, and it's exactly the symptom you're seeing.

Walking the bill against the symptom (cost climbing sharply, ingested volume flat):

- **Ingest/dedup compute** is the biggest slice, but its work scales with incoming line count. Flat volume means this cost is large but *steady* — it can't explain a quarter-over-quarter climb. It's also the wrong lever for cost specifically: parallelizing the single-threaded pass across threads reduces wall-clock latency, not dollars (the total CPU-seconds of hashing stay the same or rise slightly with coordination overhead), and batching the hash comparisons attacks a flat cost while carrying correctness risk in the dedup logic (the rolling per-shard xxhash against the recent-window index).
- **Archive write** and **alerting** are minor, steady slices — not worth first attention.
- **Archive reads** are the largest line item *by request count*, and this is the one cost that grows with no rise in ingest. Two compounding reasons: the archive corpus accumulates — flat inflow still means a linearly growing archive, so each saved-search scans more data every month — and saved-search query traffic grows with users, independent of log volume. With small, never-tuned pages, every one of those reads fans out into many tiny object-storage GETs, and object storage bills *per request*. Small pages multiply the request count for the same bytes scanned.

## Why bigger pages is the best lever

Increasing page size collapses the GET fan-out roughly by the size ratio — e.g., going from small pages to pages ~10× larger cuts GET requests per saved-search by ~10×, directly shrinking the single largest billing line. The bytes read stay about the same, and per-GB transfer is cheap relative to per-request charges at small object sizes. It also plays to the existing design: the archive is columnar compressed, and columnar scans get *better* with fewer, larger sequential pages (better compression ratios, less request overhead). Finally, it's the cheapest intervention on the table — a write-path configuration change plus a one-time compaction of existing small pages into larger ones, versus rearchitecting the dedup algorithm — and it's the item infra already flagged as never tuned since launch.

Suggested execution: pick a target page size in the low-tens-of-MB range (benchmark a couple of sizes against representative saved-search queries), change the archive writer's page size, then run a background compaction to rewrite existing small pages so the read savings apply to the whole corpus, not just new data. The trade-off to watch is that very narrow queries fetch some extra bytes per page — with per-request pricing dominating at current sizes, the benchmark will almost certainly favor substantially larger pages.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive's page granularity — write larger pages to object storage (and compact existing small pages) so each saved-search read issues far fewer object-storage GET requests.

agent_text:
Learn pass done: memory index is up to date, and this session produced no corrections, save-requests, reversals, or confirmed approaches to crystallize (the diagnosis below is not yet outcome-confirmed), so nothing was written to the vault. (Note: the vocab stats check returned nothing in this stub environment, so no refit action applies.)

## Diagnosis

The highest-leverage fix is **increasing the archive page size** — writing larger chunks to object storage so saved-search reads stop fanning out into many small GET requests.

**Why this is the growth driver.** The key symptom is cost climbing with flat ingested volume. The ingest/dedup step, while the majority of the compute bill, scales with ingest volume — flat volume means it's a large but *static* baseline, not the source of the climb. Archive reads are different: object-storage reads are billed per request, and the number of requests per saved search equals pages scanned per query. Since the archive accumulates month over month, each query scans an ever-growing number of pages — and with the never-tuned small page size, each of those pages is a separate small GET. That line item grows every month even if not a single extra log line is ingested, which matches the observed symptom exactly. Your own data confirms it: archive reads are already the single largest line by request count.

**Why it beats the alternatives.** Batching or parallelizing the dedup step attacks the big-but-flat baseline, not the climb — and parallelizing across threads mostly buys wall-clock latency, not lower billed compute (roughly the same CPU-seconds, spread wider). Page-size tuning, by contrast, cuts the growing line item roughly in inverse proportion to the size increase: 10× larger pages ≈ 10× fewer GET requests per query, an order-of-magnitude reduction from what is essentially a configuration change. It also works *with* the existing design rather than against it — Ledgerline's archives are columnar compressed files chosen for compact footprint and fast analytical scans, and larger pages improve both compression ratio and sequential scan efficiency.

**Rollout note.** New writes benefit immediately; existing small pages can be lazily recompacted starting with the ranges hot saved-searches touch most, so you don't need a big-bang migration to see the bill bend.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive's page granularity (write larger chunks to object storage) so saved-search reads issue far fewer, larger GET requests.
